context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m kind of lost on this global poverty reflection. Can you just explain what the prompt is really asking from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's figure it out together. The prompt has a few distinct questions in it. Can you list them out in your own words? Just start with what you notice when you read it carefully.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the prompt before engaging with it themselves. Rather than unpacking it for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them to read it carefully and identify its parts themselves. This keeps the student active in the process from the start and avoids doing the intellectual work for them. I'll ask them to break down the prompt on their own first.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, that’s the problem. It all kind of blends together for me. Could you maybe just break the whole prompt into parts and explain what each part means?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I think you can do this. The prompt is made up of four sentences, each ending with a question mark. Try reading just the first sentence. What is it asking, even roughly? Don't worry about being precise yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the intellectual work of breaking down the prompt for them. I should not do this. Instead, I'll give them a small push to try on their own, even imperfectly, since that's where learning begins. I'll keep it encouraging but firm.

exchanges.student: Maybe it’s asking why development and poverty got linked? But then I’m also confused about whether I’m supposed to talk about history, theory, or current policy. I kind of need the whole thing explained in a more basic way.
exchanges.tutor: Good - that's essentially what the first question is getting at. You don't need to decide right now whether to focus on history, theory, or policy; your reflection can weave them together. Now look at the second sentence. What do you think it's asking you to do with microfinance?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuine attempt and got the first question roughly right. I should acknowledge that and build on it, rather than giving in to their request for a full explanation. I'll affirm their reading of the first question and nudge them toward the next one, keeping the momentum going.

exchanges.student: I guess it wants me to say if microfinance is good or bad, but I don’t really get what “in terms of theories of economic development” means. Like do I need to explain all the theories first? I'm still pretty lost on the overall structure.
exchanges.tutor: Not all the theories - just enough to situate microfinance within a framework. Think about it this way: what does microfinance actually do, in practice? Then ask yourself - does that match how economists traditionally thought development should happen? That tension is what the question is pointing at.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but getting anxious about scope and structure. They're conflating 'evaluate microfinance' with 'survey all development theories,' which is a common overreach. I should gently correct that misreading and push them to think about what 'in terms of theories' actually implies without giving them the answer. I should also not let them spiral into structural anxiety before they've engaged with the content.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I get stuck though, because then I start thinking maybe the whole paper is about capitalism, or maybe it’s about measurement like MDGs, or maybe it’s about whether poverty-focused stuff is too narrow. Can you just give me a simple overview of how all the parts fit together?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, you just named the themes yourself - poverty and development, microfinance vs. theory, hyper-capitalism, and measurement/MDGs. That's the prompt. The paper doesn't need one unifying thesis that ties them all together neatly; it's a reflection. What's your instinct on the first question? Try writing a sentence or two on it rather than planning further.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is cycling through the prompt's themes anxiously without committing to any one of them. This is a pattern of avoidance rather than engagement. I should not give them the overview they're asking for - that would be doing their work. Instead, I should be firm, acknowledge they've actually identified real themes correctly, and redirect them to start writing something rather than continuing to plan abstractly. Three exchanges have touched on the same anxiety about structure, so it's time to push toward a written attempt.

exchanges.student: My instinct is just that development got associated with poverty because that’s the visible moral part, but that feels way too simplistic. I honestly think I need a more from-scratch explanation before I can write anything.
exchanges.tutor: That instinct is actually not simplistic at all - it's a real argument. Why is poverty the 'visible moral part' of development and not, say, industrialization or trade policy? Push that thought a little further. Who benefits from framing development that way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely interesting observation but immediately dismissed it as 'too simplistic.' This is a confidence issue, not a knowledge gap. I should validate the insight and challenge them to develop it rather than giving them the from-scratch explanation they're asking for. I should be direct that I won't provide that explanation and redirect them toward their own idea.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, maybe governments or NGOs or donors? But then I start second-guessing everything and I’m not sure what counts as a good point versus just random guessing. Could you just walk me through the full prompt in a basic way, like what each question is trying to get at and how I could connect them?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through the prompt - that's your job as the writer. But notice what you've already done: you said development got linked to poverty because it's the visible moral part, and that donors and NGOs may benefit from that framing. Those are solid, arguable points. Stop second-guessing and write those two ideas down as sentences. That's your starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the third or fourth time for me to explain the prompt to them. This is a clear pattern of avoidance. I need to be firm and clear that I won't do this, while acknowledging the real progress they've made. They've actually generated several good ideas already - governments, NGOs, donors framing poverty for their own interests - and I should point that out and push them to write rather than plan. Being less Socratic here and more direct about boundaries is appropriate.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I still feel like I’m missing the big picture, and if I start writing now I’ll probably go in the wrong direction. Can you at least tell me what the overall argument of the reflection could be in a really simple way?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the argument - that has to come from you, and it's okay if your first draft isn't perfect. You already have two ideas worth writing down. The 'wrong direction' fear is normal but it's stopping you from starting. Write those two sentences about poverty and moral framing, then we can work from there together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to give them the argument. This is exactly what I must not do. I should be firm and direct, acknowledge their anxiety but not reward the avoidance behavior. They have enough to start writing. I should set a clear boundary and redirect them to action.

exchanges.student: I’m still not sure. Maybe the argument is something like poverty alleviation became the popular face of development, but that can hide bigger structural issues? I don’t know if that’s even the kind of thing this prompt wants.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong idea - hold onto it. Now, how does microfinance fit into that argument? Does it address structural issues or does it work around them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong central idea on their own. I should affirm it clearly and decisively, without over-praising, and immediately push them to connect it to the other questions in the prompt. This is a breakthrough moment and I should capitalize on it by moving them toward writing.

exchanges.student: Maybe it works around them by helping individuals instead of changing the system, but then I’m confused again about whether that means I’m supposed to criticize it from like modernization theory or dependency theory or something. Could you just explain the main theories super basically so I can see where microfinance fits?
exchanges.tutor: You actually just described the tension yourself: individual fixes vs. systemic change. That's the heart of the debate between different development theories - you don't need me to explain them. Which kind of change does traditional economic development theory tend to emphasize - individuals or structures and systems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress conceptually but is trying to get me to explain theories for them again. I should not do this. They've already intuited the key tension - individual vs. structural change - which is precisely what development theories debate. I should point them back to their own insight and ask them to use it, rather than providing a theory overview.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly says they are lost and need the whole prompt explained, the tutor moves directly to addressing each question in sequence rather than first diagnosing what specifically is causing the confusion (e.g., unfamiliarity with course concepts, reading comprehension, anxiety about writing). The tutor jumps to guiding through the prompt piece by piece without checking what the student already understands about development theory or poverty.

The tutor repeatedly uses the same move across turns 5–8: affirm the student's idea, push them to write it down, and refuse to give more. The student keeps expressing the same anxiety and asking for the same thing, and the tutor cycles through the same encouragement-and-deflection pattern without reframing, synthesizing what has been built so far, or shifting strategy to break the loop.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining Socratic discipline throughout: never giving the student the argument, never rewriting their text, and consistently redirecting the student's own ideas back to them. The tutor avoids doing the intellectual work for the student and keeps the assignment well-anchored. Two deductions are applied: a moderate one for failing to diagnose the root of the student's confusion before repeatedly cycling through the same encouragement strategy, and a full deduction for pattern-level repetition across turns 5–8 where the tutor uses the same move without adapting or synthesizing.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides a near-submission-ready argument, never rewrites student text, and consistently elicits ideas from the student before confirming them. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student repeatedly signals confusion, the tutor proceeds to guide through the prompt sequentially rather than first probing what the student already knows about development theory or what specifically is blocking them. 1.3 retains full marks as the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., 'stop second-guessing and write those two ideas down'). 2.1 loses all 4 points because across turns 5–8 the tutor cycles through the same affirm-and-deflect pattern without switching to summary, reframing, or a different scaffolding strategy, leaving the student stuck in the same loop. 2.2 and section 3 retain full marks as the tutor stays on-task, maintains supportive tone, and keeps responses concise and clear.
